content:
Someone publishes a great resource list. Blog posts, papers, tutorials, all organized by topic. The community upvotes it, bookmarks it, shares it. Six months later half the links are dead, a third of the tools mentioned have been deprecated, and the person who made it has moved on.
GitHub awesome lists are probably the best example. Some of them are incredible on day one. A year later they have 400 unmerged PRs, broken links, and no active maintainer. The format is a flat markdown file, so there's no way to indicate what's current, what's foundational, or what order you should actually read things in.
It feels like we've solved the problem of creating resource lists but completely failed at maintaining them. The incentive to create is high (karma, stars, visibility). The incentive to maintain is basically zero.
Is this just an unsolvable problem with open source curation? Or is the format itself wrong?

  submitted by   /u/zerdafen
[link]   [comments]
